Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I'd end up scrolling through my phone or watching something, then suddenly realize I needed to cram. I have about 21 hours a week that I'd call 'study time,' but a lot of that was just me sitting with materials open without really absorbing much.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was coming up. If there was a deadline looming, I'd suddenly kick into gear, but otherwise I'd drift through the week without much structure. I'm not great with consistency—I know I should be better about it, but motivation comes and goes for me. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything until the last minute.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if something was due soon or seeing a notification that reminded me the course existed. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't actually accomplish much. If I got an email about a deadline, that would definitely prompt me to check in. Otherwise, I could go days without thinking about it.
Q4: For a brief period, yeah, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS in the mornings with my coffee. It felt kind of automatic for maybe two or three weeks. But then something threw me off—I think I had some personal stuff going on—and I never really got back into that habit. It's hard for me to maintain routines when my motivation dips.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since I learn better by reading and writing things out. I'd download PDFs and highlight stuff in my own notes. The videos were okay, but I found myself zoning out during them. Having text I could go back to and reread at my own pace worked better for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion boards most of the time. I posted a few things when required, but I didn't see the point in engaging beyond that. The optional quizzes and extra practice materials also got skipped because I figured if it wasn't graded, why bother? I know that's not a great attitude, but that's where my head was at.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly going to help me pass or if it was directly connected to an assignment. If I couldn't see an immediate payoff, I'd push it aside. I needed to feel like my effort was going somewhere concrete. Vague 'enrichment' stuff just didn't register as important to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I could skip without consequence, so I usually did. Required ones had this weight to them because I knew they'd affect my grade. Even then, I'd do the bare minimum on required stuff. The optional ones felt like suggestions rather than things I actually needed to engage with.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and work through them on my tablet without being connected. I also used some external YouTube videos to clarify concepts when the course materials were confusing. My notes app has tons of stuff that the LMS would never know about.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours working through downloaded materials or rewriting notes by hand, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd be genuinely learning, making connections, understanding things—but to the system, it probably looked like I was inactive. My best studying often happened away from the computer entirely.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a PDF once, download it, and then study from it for days without logging back in. The LMS would show one quick access, but I'd actually be reviewing that material multiple times offline. It was just easier to work with downloaded files than to keep going back to the platform.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I started feeling this general sense of 'what's the point' about everything, not just the course. My resilience isn't great, so when things got overwhelming, I kind of shut down. I'd still attend class, but engaging with the material felt like pushing through mud. The CBT sessions helped a bit with that.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed about other things—family stuff, figuring out what I'm doing with my life—the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd log in, see all the tasks, feel anxious, and then close it without doing anything. Fatigue played a role too; I'd be too tired to focus properly even when I tried.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing while doing other things, or I'd scroll through readings without actually processing them. It was like going through the motions to feel productive without actually being engaged. My mind would be elsewhere even when my eyes were on the screen.
Q15: Assignments were the big thing I'd postpone. I knew they were important, but something about starting them felt paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd push it again. It's not that I didn't care—I just couldn't make myself start. My completion rate shows that pretty clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. When a deadline was close, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, downloading everything, watching videos at 2x speed, trying to catch up. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only system that worked for me.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. Good feedback made me feel briefly motivated, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage more because it confirmed my fears about not being good enough. I'd read it, feel bad, and then avoid the course for a while. Not the most productive response, I know.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which meant I could predict when things were due and plan my procrastination accordingly. Having regular weekly modules helped somewhat, but I still struggled to keep up. The structure was there; I just didn't always follow it the way I should have.
Q19: Having all the readings available as downloadable files made things easier for me since I prefer reading offline. But the number of online courses I'm juggling—like 18 total—made it hard to give this one proper attention. The course itself wasn't badly designed; I was just spread too thin to engage properly with any single one.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in a lot, watches videos, browses around, but then can't bring themselves to actually complete and submit work. The starting is easy; the finishing is hard. There's probably anxiety or perfectionism involved—you engage with the material but freeze when it comes to producing something.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did okay on exams even though my assignment completion was low and my LMS activity was moderate. I studied offline a lot, and I'm decent at picking up concepts from readings even without heavy platform engagement. The LMS doesn't capture everything that contributes to understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture motivation, mental state, or what's happening in your life that affects your studying.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in consistent effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and invested. For me, true engagement has been rare this semester. I've been physically present but mentally checked out more often than I'd like to admit.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with motivation or how the CBT sessions helped me get through rough patches. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I felt opening the platform or the offline studying I did. It also wouldn't show that I'm dealing with a lot of uncertainty about my future, which affects how much energy I have for coursework.
